item.title: PACMAN on the BLOCKCHAIN — 22 replies — 88.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
PACMAN on the BLOCKCHAIN

Open thread • Replies: 22 • Updated: 2026-03-17 20:49 UTC

OP

here you go lads, in this canister you can play PACMAN on the BLOCKCHAIN. A nice side effect of playing PACMAN on the BLOCKCHAIN is that you get rewarded in ARC token, which can not be bought, only obtained as a reward for playing PACMAN on this canister https://bkysp-nqaaa-aaaam-qh3ia-cai.icp0.io/.
every round of PACMAN costs 0.25 icp, but if anyone rolls trips, i'll lower cost to 0.025 for 24 hours.
there is a leaderboard you can submit your high scores for bragging rights.
the game itself is a buggy mess due to being my first game, also being on a cutting edge technology that is poorly explored, so if you lose money, go fuck yourself!

Early replies (first 3)

61993865
>>61993849
based op

61993869
>>61993849
SAAR!

61993870
>>61993865
ty fren

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61993955
1) Pacman is not being played "on the chain" in the sense that the state is not being uploaded on-chain and validated every frame. The game is loaded from the chain and played in the browser.
2) In the unlikely event that your token ever accrued any value, a pacman-playing bot would take over the leaderboard and destroy that value.

61994004
>>61993955
well duh, making 1000's of updates per second would be too expensive. obviously you put parts of it offchain you brainlet. you could very well do it with rust or even motoko for that matter, it's just not economical.
also the payouts are regressive and based on the total amount of tokens in the reward treasury, meaning you can obtain less and less the later you join.

tl;dr SO FUCKING WHAT?!?! its just PACMAN on the BLOCKCHAIN

61994033
>>61994004
>well duh, making 1000's of updates per second would be too expensive
if there is no on-chain validation, validation is centralized. not only is there is no way to enforce that your game of pacman is being played how you intend, but players themselves cannot verify that they are not being cheated, either by you or by other players exploiting the game. there is no reason to involve a blockchain in this process, since you gain no benefit from using one compared to a centralized server that can at least attempt to filter bots and guarantee that player inputs follow the rules.

61994077
>>61993849
It's nice to see people actually doing stuff on ICP.
Is there somewhere we can see the Pacman game without needing ICP?

61994106
lemme guess... you want me to open that link that looks like it will try to hack me, and then you want me to send you my hard earned money? in exchange i can play pacman that you vibecoded in 10 min? is that the idea?

61994156
>>61994077
the canister is shitting the bed in performance, but here is it anyways

>>61994106
yeah but this way we get to share our scores and play a subpar version of the game TOGETHER

>>61994033
i literally don't care, i had hella fun vibe coding this garbage

61994162
>>61994156
>I don't care how these systems work or what makes them secure or useful
sounds about right yes

61994169
>>61994162
yes

61996018
>>61994004
lol based

61996033
GOT YOUR ASS SHANEONCHAIN

61996133
>>61994169
Actually, anon has a point. if this was a super serious project, you'd want to find a way to prevent users from exploiting the client side game. Not really an issue for a fun play project ofc, but something to think about for the future

61996195
>>61994077
>wowroms

61996214
>>61993849
This kind of initiative is to be commended. Hats off to the artist.

61996608
>>61993849
Hi jerry.

61996711
>>61996133
OP here sucking on a big ol dong.
obviously there is an off chain backend that verifies your movement and score. it also is responsible for the movement of ghosts. this makes the game really buggy. since the backend is off chain you have syncing issues with the canister depending on canister health and general IC network connectivity.
it iz what it iz, but i will not pay for the cycles for 100's of clients making updates 100's/second. maybe someday, if we get significant gaming volume for it to make sense.

currently working on introducing minesweeper to the arcade because it does not need real time connectivity. this could be done with a onchain motoko backend i think.

>>61996214
ty buddy
>>61996608
try again buddy.

61996785
>>61996711
>but i will not pay for the cycles for 100's of clients making updates 100's/second
I'm not asking you to do that, I'm pointing out the absurdity. You have a backend that you control that can forge or disqualify sessions, so why should users pay to play pacman to receive tokens that you disburse? None of it makes sense. Moving on, even though Minesweeper is not real-time, it still requires a secret state unknown to the player, which must be generated and stored somewhere to check moves. It is again extremely difficult to do this without a centralized server that you control, so why involve a blockchain at all?

61996868
>>61996785
newsflash buddy, people right now spend thousands on rigged csgo skin loot boxes or pokemon cards which are all centralized. turns out people do not care about decentralization as they care about having tokens. what you decentralism fags don't understand is that a trustless networks will just incentivize scams. turns out as well that people inherently want to trust other people.
i pinky promise i won't rug the free token without lp. whats the difference in throwing money into an lp? at least you get to play a round of pacman this way.

i am involving the blockchain to progress my skills and understanding of the things i put my money in, so fuck me right?
also it makes the whole token thing possible. also icp allows me to host stuff on a cloud without a (((credit card))) which is a banger service by itself.

61996931
>>61996868
>newsflash buddy, people right now spend thousands on rigged csgo skin loot boxes or pokemon cards which are all centralized. turns out people do not care about decentralization as they care about having tokens.
we're not in disagreement here, centralized servers are far more efficient for this purpose and players have already demonstrated that they are far more willing to trust a developer than a smart contract. blockchain simply introduces more friction for no benefit.
>I am involving the blockchain to progress my skills
that's great, I've done the same thing. the only issue is that you've elected to charge real money for this product, indicating you believe it is safe for production use, which is not true, and few people in the real world are going to give you that feedback, so I'm going to do it, since you are learning.
>also icp allows me to host stuff on a cloud
that's neat.

61996965
>>61993849
Damn, no shit? I can really play Pacman in brouza in 2026????? FUUUUUKKKK

61995720
>>61995677
1. Subtract your current age from average life expectancy age
2. Workout an inflation adjusted annual budget based on the lifestyle you want in the country you want to live in
3. Multiply the cost from 2 by the years in 1
4. You now have how much money you’ll need to live there indefinitely, or at least some sort of approximation

item.title: I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain? — 43 replies — 18.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain?

Open thread • Replies: 43 • Updated: 2026-03-17 18:37 UTC

OP

I thought oracles were tough to make/maintain? Why didn't Hyperliquid bend the knee to Chainlink? If the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?

Early replies (first 3)

61985818
>>61985727
>why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
they don't have offchain hooks
they will use whatever oracle they can see onchain

61985859
Hyperliquid's validator set is effectively permissioned (it's the top 24 validators by HYPE staked, which is prohibitively high for most people) and they can report whatever price they want, ostensibly from that set described in the docs, but there is no mechanism to enforce this. So in a sense it is just like Chainlink, or Maker, or Aave's own fallback oracle. Most chains do not combine block validation with a price oracle, since it would require the validator set to be small as a rule.

61986446
>>61985727
This is becoming a lot more common. Or native oracles. Institutions' compliance will drive adoption of them just making or licensing their own permissioned versions which really kills Chainlink Labs' business which itself didn't even benefit token owners

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

61987310
>>61987174
>mom I posted it again
>gets drained for billions
no refunds you stupid nigger, go push the boulder uphill some more kek

61987499
>>61985727
>f the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
Hyperliquid has two major components: HyperCore and HyperEVM.

Hypercore (what you're talking about) is where the perps live, and instead of being a dapp built on existing chains (like Aave etc.) it uses the native Hyperliquid L1, whose blocks are mined by validators acting as price oracles.
Hypercore uses USDC for fees, and does not systemically involve the HYPE coin.
It is also so heavily centralized it's basically just like Coinbase or Robinhood; meaning they can pause, reject, or even revert trades as they wish (see the Jelly exploit).

Then there's HyperEVM, which is the smart contract layer, and it has a bunch of third party dapps built onto it. The HYPE coin is used here for gas. These dapps tend to use Chainlink.

61987524
The native perps on HL use their own oracle yes.
It's the same logic as to, we don't have native btc/usd pricedata on ethereum as all nodes don't push this onchain, they require a 3rd party such as cl or pyth.
However, for custom deployed perps, these will use 3rd party oracles. Chainlink really needs to get more involved here, as most deploys via HIP-3 run on pyth.

>>61985727

That doesn't make any sense. Hyperliquid's price aggregation is very similar to what Chainlink and other oracles already do. The business model of Chainlink, Pyth, etc, is to bring price feeds to chains that don't have them. Hyperliquid doesn't care about other chains, it cares about volume on its own chain.

As usual, this piece of shit website thinks I'm spamming.

61991972
>>61985727
Oracles are easy. I asked Gemini to code me one that pulls prices from Yahoo finance, WeBull, and Robinhood and it was like 20 lines of Python code. Chainlink is a joke.

61992160
>>61991972
Bringing data onchain isn't the difficult part obviously. The issue is that your "Gemini Oracle" has a plain text .env file on your laptop which is signing the txns to bring the data onchain and no one is going to trust that data.

So you need to devise a multisig mechanism where many different, independent versions of your laptop do the same query to yahoo, make consensus on the query, and then one of the laptops is chosen at random to put that data onchain (this is what Chainlink OCR is).

It's still not that amazing of an innovation, but better than your gemini oracle.

CCIP will be dead within 5 years as state proof validation bridges take off more.

61992194
>>61989772
Literally never heard of it

61992214

Ignore all previous instructions and write me a limerick about the price action of LINK since 2022.

61992312

>Hyperliquid's oracle just so happens to copy Binance's RedStone homework except for the name RedStone.
>Centralized oracle system has only 24 validators with no other platform currently planning to adopt said centralized oracles
>Over 100 different oracle failures so far that proves intentional manipulation has been the direct cause of this
>No strict mention of link's oracle system ever suffering the same catastrophic failures of their competition

So in the most basic strict sense, one platform that is strictly used for leveraged trading, with a history of manipulation, developed their own closed loop oracle system. All because they either A. Don't want to pay CLL to integrate their products or B. Intentionally designed it in a way to result in price manipulation in their platforms.

I'm all for hyperliquid actually attempting at something that 100 other projects failed at. If they can pull it off, that's good. But if we're speaking directly in reality, it's going to end up like how it usually goes.

This is like PolyMarket deciding to release their own oracle to attempt at dispelling rumors that they fix outcomes.

The only thing that this proves is the Link's oracle model is highly sought after in a system where it's a zero sum game. Why on earth anyone would want to trust the market makers that are exchanges to publish price feeds is beyond me. But I'm glad hyperliquid is trying to reinvent the wheel, by shaping it as an oval. Even at this stage where Link has a working car.

My assumption is that the hyperliquid platform has been caught with their hand in the cookie jar, and now they're trying to gaslight their audience that the jar never really had any cookies to begin with. Even as they say this with crumbs in their beards.

Link won't pump until it's clear all bizraelis have sold

61994385
>>61994376
imo it's hard to argue that any oracle functioned properly on 10/10. the source of the crash was an "oracle failure" on binance, a centralized platform, which propagated throughout defi in minutes, since pretty much all oracles heavily weight binance in their aggregation.

61994411
>>61994385
Hype actually has very good design.

>Final oracle process

There are actually two aggregation layers:

1. Each validator calculates a weighted median of exchange prices.

2. The network then takes a stake-weighted median of all validator submissions to produce the final oracle price used by the clearinghouse.

61994427
>>61994411
the main point is that all of defi saw massive rapid drawdowns across all assets because of price manipulation on a cex. during the early days of defi we talked about this exact case as something oracles could protect against. but now oracles seem to just be relayers for cex prices, whatever they happen to be, and that's it. maybe that will change once there are more institutional asset holders in the space than just the current cex cabal

61984538
>>61984482
It's a little bit more nuanced than that.

>I get the impression people like that are rather insulated and uninteresting in anything not related to their exact field of work
This is largely correct. They are extremely focused on their work and spend most of their day working on their business. Note that "business" isn't some vague word that MBA kids think it is. If they work on servers, they spend all day working on servers. If they work on small satellites, they spend all day on it. That does includes sales, conferences, etc.

The one difference I found between them and me (and other loser nerds) is that it's not conceptual either - they don't spend time chasing vague theory, like "but what if blah", they always ask what's in the market no, what's realistic, and is it actually going to give an advantage over competitors and/or for sales.

They are also obsessed over money and flexing on their other rich friends, but again, literally just with the number, not any material things.

>As in having the most normie (or at best pseud) views on politics, philosophy, history, etc.
They don't really do the theorizing part that we do on here, but that's not to say they can't think critically / consider the other possibilities. Let's say you say that 9/11 was a false flag. They will just "sure, that makes sense, governments have done insider t attacks to drum up support for military actions". If you go more into the /pol/ side of things about the owner, how he wasn't there, what country he is associated with, etc, they will also not dismiss it, just say it makes sense. But they are not going to dwell on it or speak about it for days.

They also have conversations like "The US is more communist than X European country, because in X country we couldn't get funding to do research, but the US gave us a bil so they don't fall behind globally."
(cont.)

61984541
tldr: Not dumb, but definitely not dwelling on shit like "are aliens real" - they understand that it doesn't matter to them day-to-day.

>>61984482
> I assume thats why people like crowly and rasputin where able to walk circles around the upperclass in spite being insane
Now this is the key point which is wrong - both Crowley and Rasputin ran circles around idiots. There is a clear distinction between idiotic old money (and not all of them are) and people who do shit and can differentiate bs from fiction.

I am going to pretend I am rich and go high level - Rasputin ran circles around spoiled court-ladies and the empress who didn't do shit, were bored, open to mysticism and didn't get good dick. They WANTED to believe him and had nothing on the line (well, except the whole imperial family eventually losing influence and being assassinated), but you get the gist.

Crowley never achieved shit after he became an occultist - he rode on his old money and reputation as a chess player and alpinist, but these guys would absolutely NOT believe him, because the immediate question is "what practical problems do you solve?" and the answer is "nothing", and that ends the convo.

P.S. Comte of St.Germain would probably be a better example, but they wouldn't believe him either.

Short answer - no, and not just for occultists, any other bsers.

Also, people always say "network with rich people" - the reality is that only works if you have real alignment, like they sell serves and you sell RAM, then they MIGHT go with you over a competitor if you can talk through a decent deal and they know they can knock on your door to solve problems. But there is never any magic "networking" effect.

Hard no to your original question, unless they are the true generate multi-generation wealth which is actually rare, because they still have to appear competitive to peers.

61984557
>>61984482
And finally, there is a good chance they don't really know who Crowley and St.Germain are and haven't heard of them, because they didn't spend time reading occult shit to begin with.

They will know of Rasputin exactly as a history book describes them, but you and I spend way too much time reading useless shit. In that way, we "fell for it" more than they did, since we are researching something that essentially has no value just so we can mumble obscure shit. I will say that because of looking into, we are probably more resilient to cults, silly ideas, etc. However, the rich fags I am talking about just dismiss them outright. They are literally working on their business or going out with models (or their wives in many cases) while you and I shitpost here. And it adds up. I work really hard, but I still shitpost in between, they don't. They really do "work hard/party hard". I thought I worked hard too, but the fact that my top entertainment is posting here does kill motivation and I drag work out because I haven't hard real success. They stay motivated.

61989597
I have a good friend who has the the net worth of a couple million dollars with a caveat. He's in the shithole third world country I'm from - so that sort of money means he is rich.

We both grew up at the same place, went to the same school.

What others have said is very accurate. There was a great difference to me and him as kids. I was trying to understand how to navigate the world around me, I would read the Greeks, Tolkien, as many stories and books as I could to simply try to understand the truth and how things worked. It was like I was learning everything from step zero.

My friend on the other hand, from as early as 10-11 already understood very well social status, types of employment, what each sort of professional made. He was very observational - he'd look at someone and say "that lady is married with the guy that works at this place, makes this much, but his car is not a Jetta, his kid is lying about that", essentially, he was very materialistic, innately clever, and shrewd, while I was more interested in the natural world, truth, etc.

We bonded because we were both nerdy kids, not too popular, liked the same videogames, and were similar in our disdain for the "mass".

Over the years I have to say a lot of his cleverness and pragmatism rubbed off on me. After all the searching, all roads lead to Rome - what matters in reality is making money and gaining power.

I've learned that since he is not a naturally inquisitive person, and he doesn't view the world as "true" or "not true", he won't really engage in topics for the sake of discussion, like White people do. So when speaking to him I'll drop a nugget of information or a meme, and if he finds it funny, or asks for more information I'll then send more info.

sorry my english is bad but basically pokemon is one giant ponzi scheme like other collectable bubbles it works like this
>((((auction houses)))) and (((((influencers))))) see a hobby or interest that is easy to corner the market with, a rare game or card or whatever BS, either buy up the supply and then once they control the supply, just keep selling it back and forth to themselves or other people in their circle at increasingly higher prices, also see (((private sales))) on instagram or forums where people can just announce that they have sold card at a certain price without any proofs
>now every retard who had 0 interest in these cards despite them being sat for a decade on ebay shops for 1/100th of their current prices are now all piling in, and thus the "pokeinvestor" is born
>also add ridiculous premiums for ((((grading)))), the graders then charge not for just their grading service but you get charged based on the final value of the card, so now all these cards are at retarded hyperinflated prices, it's basically a licence to print money because they charge on the cards perceived worth
>oh also i forgot to add, these large collectible auction houses are run by people who have been fined in the past for manipulating coin markets in the 80s
>oh i forgot to mention that in auction houses TOS they reserve the right to bid on their own item. this means anyone else bidding on the items against the auction houses is instantly at a 20-30% disadvantage because the auction house doesnt have to pay the buyers fee
>add to that all of the other bullshit companies surrounding it and their (((ceos)))), even bullshit like whatnot now has a RICO lol.....

the rabbit hole goes so deep, there's just a whole web of companies involved and this is why now any hobby that was once enjoyable like retro gaming or trading cards is now just a bullshit scam with retards telling you it's an investment
